children.name: Natrix
children.description: The water snakes of Europe — non-venomous, excellent swimmers, and important indicators of healthy wetland ecosystems. The Grass Snake is Sweden's most common snake and one of the most frequently encountered reptiles near water. Its distinctive white-and-yellow collar and ability to feign death convincingly make it instantly recognisable.

children.name: Natrix natrix
children.description: Sweden's largest snake; non-venomous; eats frogs and fish; feigns death when threatened; lays eggs in compost heaps for warmth; the white-yellow collar behind the head is the key field mark.

children.name: Natrix maura
children.description: Mimics adder patterning as defence; highly aquatic; Mediterranean and NW Africa.

children.name: Natrix tessellata
children.description: Almost entirely aquatic; river specialist; checker-patterned dorsum.

children.name: Natrix helvetica
children.description: Split from Natrix natrix in 2017; found in western Europe including France, Switzerland, and western Germany. Distinguished by darker, more contrasting colouration on the flanks.

children.name: Natrix astreptiphora
children.description: A grass snake of Morocco, Algeria, and Tunisia. Adapted to the semi-arid conditions of the Maghreb, often found near oases and permanent water sources in otherwise dry landscapes.

children.name: Coronella
children.description: The smooth snake of Europe.

children.name: Coronella austriaca
children.description: Sweden's rarest snake; legally protected; constricts lizards and other snakes as prey; viviparous; often mistaken for the Common Adder and killed; declining due to habitat fragmentation.

children.name: Pantherophis
children.description: North American ratsnakes.

children.name: Pantherophis guttatus
children.description: The world's most popular pet snake; docile temperament; stunning natural colour polymorphism; hundreds of captive morphs; excellent rodent controller.

children.name: Pantherophis obsoletus
children.description: Large constrictor of eastern woodlands; excellent climber; up to 2.4 m.

children.name: Pantherophis alleghaniensis
children.description: A large North American constrictor, reaching up to 2.5 metres. An excellent climber, often found in barns and attics hunting rodents. Variable in colour, from black in the north to yellow with dark stripes in the south.

children.name: Pantherophis spiloides
children.description: A greyish ratsnake of the central and southeastern United States. Juveniles have a distinctive pattern of dark blotches that fade as they mature. Important controller of rodent populations in agricultural areas.

children.name: Pantherophis ramspotti
children.description: Found in the Great Lakes region of the US and Canada. Prefers wetlands and prairies. When threatened, vibrates its tail and releases a musk that smells like fox urine — hence the common name.

children.name: Pantherophis vulpinus
children.description: A large, non-venomous constrictor found in the eastern Great Lakes region. Distinctive copper-coloured head and dark blotches on a tan body. Threatened by habitat loss and road mortality.

children.name: Pantherophis bairdi
children.description: A colourful ratsnake of the southwestern US and northern Mexico. The body is yellow-orange with four dark longitudinal stripes. Found in rocky canyons and cliff faces.

children.name: Lampropeltis
children.description: King snakes immune to viper venom.

children.name: Lampropeltis getula
children.description: Immune to rattlesnake venom; actively hunts and kills other snakes including pit vipers; bold black-and-white bands.

children.name: Lampropeltis triangulum
children.description: Mimics coral snake colouring in southern populations; wide range from Canada to Ecuador.

children.name: Lampropeltis exoteniz
children.description: A highly variable kingsnake of the southwestern US and Mexico. Colour and pattern vary geographically from banded to striped to nearly solid. An important constrictor in desert ecosystems.

children.name: Lampropeltis californiae
children.description: A popular pet snake native to California and surrounding states. Exhibits striking pattern polymorphism: banded, striped, or partially patterned individuals. Immune to rattlesnake venom and an active hunter of other snakes.

children.name: Lampropeltis ruthveni
children.description: A rare Mexican kingsnake restricted to the pine-oak forests of Michoacán. Named after herpetologist Alexander Grant Ruthven. Feeds primarily on lizards and small mammals.

children.name: Lampropeltis alterna
children.description: A strikingly marked kingsnake of the Chihuahuan Desert in Texas and Mexico. Has broad bands of orange, red, or yellow separated by grey. Highly sought after by reptile enthusiasts for its beautiful colouration.

children.name: Lampropeltis pyromelana
children.description: A tricolour kingsnake of the southwestern US and Mexico, with red, black, and white bands. Mimics the venomous coral snake. Found in high-elevation pine and oak forests.

children.name: Lampropeltis zonager
children.description: A red, black, and white banded kingsnake from the Great Plains of the United States. Often confused with coral snakes but distinguishable by the red-black-yellow banding order.

children.name: Elaphe
children.description: European and Asian ratsnakes.

children.name: Elaphe quatuorlineata
children.description: Europe's largest snake at up to 2.5 m; constrictors of Mediterranean maquis; protected across most of range.

children.name: Elaphe dione
children.description: Steppe and farmland ratsnake of Central Asia.

children.name: Elaphe schrenckii
children.description: A large, robust ratsnake of Northeast Asia, found in Russia, China, and Korea. Adults are dark with a yellow or white pattern of transverse bands. An important rodent predator in its native forests.

children.name: Elaphe carinata
children.description: A large, keel-scaled ratsnake found in Taiwan and southern China. Known for its aggressive temperament. The keeled scales give it a rough texture, distinguishing it from other Elaphe species.

children.name: Elaphe taeniura
children.description: A large, strikingly patterned ratsnake found across East and Southeast Asia. Highly variable geographically, with different subspecies sporting distinct colour patterns. A popular species in the pet trade due to its docile nature and impressive size.

children.name: Elaphe climacophora
children.description: A large, non-venomous snake endemic to Japan. Reaches up to 2.5 metres. Known as 'aodaishō' in Japanese. Particularly famous on the island of Yakushima, where it is abundant and unusually tame.

children.name: Ptyas
children.description: Oriental ratsnakes.

children.name: Ptyas mucosa
children.description: Fast-moving diurnal hunter; up to 3.5 m; common near human settlement across South and SE Asia.

children.name: Ptyas korros
children.description: A large, fast-moving diurnal snake found across South and Southeast Asia. Reaches up to 2 metres. Often found in agricultural areas where it preys on rodents. Protected across much of its range.

children.name: Ptyas dhumnates
children.description: The largest snake in the genus Ptyas, reaching up to 3.7 metres. Found across India and Southeast Asia. Despite its size, it is non-venomous and relatively docile. A very fast and agile hunter.

children.name: Thamnophis
children.description: Garter snakes — the most widespread and familiar snakes of North America, found from the Arctic Circle to Central America. All are viviparous (giving live birth) and most have the characteristic three longitudinal stripes. They are non-venomous but possess a mild Duvernoy's gland secretion toxic to some prey.

children.name: Thamnophis sirtalis
children.description: The most widespread reptile in North America, found from the Arctic to Central America. Highly adaptable, inhabiting forests, grasslands, and urban areas. Known for its distinctive longitudinal stripes and for giving birth to live young.

children.name: Thamnophis elegans
children.description: A highly variable garter snake of western North America. Some populations are semiaquatic while others are fully terrestrial. Known for its varied diet including slugs, earthworms, fish, and even small mammals.

children.name: Thamnophis radix
children.description: A common garter snake of the central United States and Canada. One of the most cold-tolerant snake species, active at temperatures below 10°C. Often found near water in prairie and agricultural landscapes.

children.name: Thamnophis marcianus
children.description: A striking garter snake with a distinctive checkerboard pattern. Found in the southwestern US, Mexico, and Central America. Semiaquatic, prefers permanent water sources in otherwise arid regions.

children.name: Thamnophis hammondii
children.description: A semiaquatic garter snake of California and Baja California. Has only two longitudinal stripes instead of the typical three. Highly aquatic, feeds almost exclusively on fish and amphibians.

children.name: Coluber
children.description: Typical racers — fast, slender, diurnal snakes of the Northern Hemisphere. Among the fastest snakes in the world, some capable of bursts over 6 km/h.

children.name: Coluber canus
children.description: A fast-moving racer of Central Asia. Pale grey colouration provides camouflage in the arid landscapes of Kazakhstan and Uzbekistan. One of the fastest snakes in its region.

children.name: Diadophis
children.description: Ring-necked snakes — small, secretive, mildly venomous rear-fanged snakes found across North America. The bright yellow, orange, or red neck ring and matching belly are the defining features.

children.name: Diadophis punctatus
children.description: A small, secretive snake found across most of the United States and Mexico. Distinctive yellow or orange ring behind the head. Brightly coloured belly displayed when threatened. Mildly venomous, with rear fangs.

children.name: Opheodrys
children.description: North American green snakes — slender, bright green, entirely insectivorous snakes. The Rough Green Snake is arboreal while the Smooth Green Snake prefers meadows. Their colour provides exceptional camouflage among leaves.

children.name: Opheodrys aestivus
children.description: A slender, bright green snake found in the southeastern United States and Mexico. Arboreal, spending most of its time in trees and shrubs. Excellent camouflage among green foliage. Feeds on insects and spiders.

children.name: Opheodrys vernalis
children.description: A small, bright green snake found across North America from Canada to Mexico. Unlike the Rough Green Snake, it has smooth scales. Prefers moist meadows and grasslands. Feeds primarily on insects.

children.name: Heterodon
children.description: Hognose snakes — famous for their theatrical defensive displays including neck spreading, hissing, and playing dead. The upturned snout is used to dig up toads, their primary prey. Rear-fanged with mild venom.

children.name: Heterodon platirhinos
children.description: The eastern hog-nosed snake, is a species of mildly venomous rear-fanged snake in the family Colubridae. The venom is specifically adapted to amphibian prey and is harmless to humans. Some people may have an allergic reaction, though, so experience local swelling and other symptoms.

children.name: Heterodon nasicus
children.description: A popular pet snake native to the Great Plains of North America. The upturned rostral scale is used for digging up toads, its primary prey. Mildly venomous rear-fanged snake, but not dangerous to humans.

children.name: Heterodon simus
children.description: The smallest of the three North American hognose snakes, found in the southeastern US. Prefers sandy, well-drained soils where it can dig for its preferred prey: Fowler's toads. Declining across its range.

children.name: Pituophis
children.description: Pine snakes and gopher snakes — large, powerful constrictors of North America. They produce one of the loudest hisses of any snake, generated by a modified epiglottis, and are important rodent predators.

children.name: Pituophis melanoleucus
children.description: Pituophis melanoleucus, commonly known as the eastern pine snake, is a species of nonvenomous snake in the family Colubridae. The species is native to the southeastern United States. Three subspecies are traditionally recognized as being valid, though taxonomic changes may be occurring.

children.name: Pituophis catenifer
children.description: Pituophis catenifer is a species of nonvenomous colubrid snake endemic to North America. Nine subspecies are currently recognized, including the nominotypical subspecies, P. catenifer, described here.

children.name: Pituophis deppei
children.description: A Mexican pine snake found in the highlands of central Mexico. Reaches up to 2 metres. Prefers pine-oak forests at elevations of 2,000 to 3,000 metres.

children.name: Rhinocheilus
children.description: Long-nosed snakes — striking banded snakes of arid North America with a pointed snout adapted for digging. When threatened they writhe and smear blood and musk from the cloaca.

children.name: Rhinocheilus antonii
children.description: A strikingly banded snake of the southwestern US and Mexico. The pointed snout is used for digging in loose soil after lizards and their eggs. Nocturnal and secretive. When threatened, it writhes and releases foul-smelling musk and blood from the cloaca.

children.name: Arizona
children.description: Glossy snakes — medium-sized, smooth-scaled constrictors of arid North America. Their glossy, iridescent scales give them their name. Nocturnal, found in sandy deserts and grasslands.

children.name: Arizona elegans
children.description: A medium-sized, smooth-scaled snake of the arid southwestern US and Mexico. Its glossy appearance and pale colouration provide excellent camouflage on sandy soils. Nocturnal, preying on lizards and small mammals.

children.name: Hypsiglena
children.description: Night snakes — small, secretive, rear-fanged venomous snakes of North America. Nocturnal, with vertically elliptical pupils. Their mild venom helps subdue lizards, their primary prey.

children.name: Hypsiglena torquata
children.description: A small, secretive, rear-fanged snake of western North America. Nocturnal and venomous, though its venom is mild and not dangerous to humans. Preys on lizards, frogs, and small snakes.

children.name: Trimorphodon
children.description: Lyre snakes — rear-fanged, mildly venomous, nocturnal snakes of the southwestern US and Central America. Named for the V-shaped lyre pattern on the crown of the head.

children.name: Trimorphodon biscutatus
children.description: A rear-fanged venomous snake of the southwestern US and Central America. Named for the V-shaped lyre pattern on its head. Nocturnal and secretive, feeding primarily on lizards.

children.name: Gyalopion
children.description: Hook-nosed snakes — small, secretive colubrids of the southwestern US and Mexico. The upturned snout is used to dig after centipedes and spiders. Rear-fanged with mild venom.

children.name: Gyalopion canum
children.description: A small, secretive snake of the Chihuahuan Desert. The upturned snout is used for digging after centipedes and spiders. Rear-fanged with mild venom. One of the smallest colubrids in North America.

children.name: Farancia
children.description: Mud snakes and rainbow snakes — highly aquatic, iridescent colubrids of the southeastern United States. Their glossy, patterned bodies are adapted for burrowing in mud and swimming through blackwater swamps.

children.name: Farancia abacura
children.description: A glossy, iridescent black snake with a pinkish-red belly and snout, found in wetlands of the southeastern US. Known as the 'hoop snake' in folklore. Highly aquatic, feeding almost exclusively on eel-like amphiumas and sirens.

children.name: Farancia erytrogramma
children.description: One of the most beautiful North American snakes, with three red and yellow stripes running down a glossy black body. Highly aquatic, found in rivers and swamps of the southeastern US. Feeds on American eels.

children.name: Carphophis
children.description: Worm snakes — tiny, pinkish-brown fossorial snakes that closely resemble earthworms. Spend nearly their entire lives underground or under leaf litter, emerging only at night or after heavy rain.

children.name: Carphophis vernis
children.description: A tiny, pinkish-brown snake that resembles an earthworm. Spends most of its life underground or under leaf litter. Feeds primarily on earthworms and soft-bodied insect larvae. Completely harmless.

children.name: Contophanes
children.description: Sharp-tailed snakes — small, secretive snakes of North and Central America. The tail ends in a sharp spine that may help anchor the snake when capturing prey. Feed on slugs and earthworms.

children.name: Contophanes tenuis
children.description: A small, secretive snake of the Pacific Coast. The tail ends in a sharp, pointed scale that may help anchor the snake when capturing prey. Feeds primarily on slugs and earthworms.

children.name: Rhabdophis
children.description: Keelback snakes — a genus of rear-fanged colubrids in Asia. Some species sequester toxins from ingested toads in specialised nuchal glands for defence. Several species cause medically significant envenomations.

children.name: Rhabdophis tigrinus
children.description: A rear-fanged colubrid native to East Asia. Uniquely, it stores toxins from ingested toads in its nuchal glands for defensive use. Causes significant envenomations in Japan, where it is considered medically important.

children.name: Rhabdophis subminiatus
children.description: A highly venomous rear-fanged colubrid found across Southeast Asia. Despite its beauty, it can deliver a medically significant bite that causes severe bleeding. The red patch behind the head is a distinctive field mark.

children.name: Boiga
children.description: Cat snakes — a large genus of rear-fanged, mildly venomous, arboreal snakes from Asia and Australia. Named for their cat-like vertical pupils. Nocturnal hunters of birds, lizards, and small mammals.

children.name: Boiga dendrophila
children.description: A large, rear-fanged, mildly venomous cat snake found in Southeast Asia. Glossy black body with bright yellow bands. Nocturnal, arboreal, and often found near water. Popular in zoos despite its defensive temperament.

children.name: Boiga irregularis
children.description: Notorious as one of the most damaging invasive species in history. Accidentally introduced to Guam after WWII, it has caused the extinction of most of the island's native birds, lizards, and bats. Rear-fanged and mildly venomous.

children.name: Dispholidus
children.description: Boomslangs — a genus of highly venomous African tree snakes. The word 'boomslang' means 'tree snake' in Afrikaans. Their potent haemotoxic venom causes fatal internal bleeding, though they are shy and bites are rare.

children.name: Dispholidus semivittatus
children.description: A highly venomous African colubrid with potent haemotoxic venom that causes fatal internal bleeding. Largely arboreal with exceptional camouflage. Despite its danger, bites are rare as it is shy and avoids humans. Large eyes with distinctive keeled scales.

children.name: Thelotornis
children.description: Twig snakes — slender, highly venomous rear-fanged African snakes that closely mimic twigs and branches. Also known as vine snakes. Their camouflage is exceptional, and their venom causes severe bleeding disorders.

children.name: Thelotornis capensis
children.description: A slender, highly venomous rear-fanged snake that mimics twigs and branches. Its camouflage is so effective that it is often overlooked until touched. The venom causes severe bleeding disorders.

children.name: Spalerosophis
children.description: Diadem snakes — medium to large constrictors of arid Asia and North Africa. Named for the distinctive crown-like or diadem pattern on the head. Fast-moving diurnal hunters of rodents.

children.name: Spalerosophis atriceps
children.description: A beautiful colubrid of Central Asia and the Middle East. The dark head contrasts with a pale body marked with dark blotches. A fast-moving constrictor that preys on rodents, common in arid rocky habitats.

children.name: Hemorrhous
children.description: Whip snakes — fast, diurnal, long-bodied colubrids of Europe and Africa. Named for their slender, whip-like bodies. Arboreal hunters that pursue lizards and small birds with great speed.

children.name: Hemorrhous hippocrepis
children.description: A fast, diurnal colubrid of the Iberian Peninsula and North Africa. Named for the characteristic horseshoe-shaped mark on the back of the head. Arboreal and an active hunter of lizards and small birds.
